misconceptions.claim: The verifier must somehow look at all 2^n terms (or trust the prover's single number H) — there is no middle ground.
misconceptions.why_tempting: A sum is defined as adding everything up, so 'verify the sum' feels like it must mean 'redo the addition.' The whole point of sum-check is the middle ground: the verifier never adds the 2^n terms, yet is not reduced to blind trust — it cross-examines one variable per round.

misconceptions.claim: Each round the verifier checks the prover's claimed univariate g_i against the TRUE univariate, so it must already know the answer.
misconceptions.why_tempting: The check g_i(0)+g_i(1) = (running claim) looks like comparing to a known truth. In fact the verifier only checks an internal consistency identity — it never computes the true partial sum — and defers all trust to the final single evaluation, which is the only thing it checks against g directly.

misconceptions.claim: A cheating prover who is caught in one round can simply repair its lie in the next round and keep going.
misconceptions.why_tempting: Each round looks independent, so a liar seems to get fresh attempts. The inductive soundness argument kills this: to pass round i the prover must send a wrong univariate that still satisfies the consistency check, and Schwartz–Zippel makes the random r_i expose that wrongness with high probability — a lie must survive EVERY round, and the failure probabilities add up to only d·v/|F|.

misconceptions.claim: Sum-check needs a polynomial commitment (or some cryptographic primitive) to be sound.
misconceptions.why_tempting: Every other proof system the reader meets later leans on commitments, so sum-check looks incomplete without one. But sum-check is a PURE interactive reduction: assuming oracle access to g, its soundness is unconditional (Schwartz–Zippel), needing no commitment, no group, no hardness assumption. The commitment only enters later when g itself must be pinned down.

misconceptions.claim: The random challenge r_i is chosen to make the math convenient, like a clever substitution.
misconceptions.why_tempting: Plugging in a specific value feels like a simplification trick. The randomness is the soundness engine: r_i must be UNPREDICTABLE to the prover so a wrong univariate cannot have been tuned to agree at r_i — exactly the Schwartz–Zippel move (a low-degree non-zero difference is non-zero at a random point with high probability).

misconceptions.claim: The soundness error is per round, so n rounds means the protocol is n times less sound — it gets worse fast.
misconceptions.why_tempting: Compounding usually means multiplying small badness into big badness. Here the per-round errors ADD (union bound): each round risks ≤ d/|F|, so the total is ≤ d·v/|F| — linear in the number of variables, not exponential, and still tiny for a large field.

misconceptions.claim: Sum-check reveals the witness, so it is already a zero-knowledge proof.
misconceptions.why_tempting: It is built from the same algebra as the ZK machinery and 'narrows a confession,' so it sounds like it hides things. By itself sum-check is NOT zero-knowledge: the prover reveals genuine evaluations of g; masking polynomials (Ch 9–10) are needed to make it ZK. Conflating the two corrupts the later security models.

dumb_questions: If the verifier never adds the 2^n terms, what stops the prover from just announcing any H it likes?
Why does the prover send a whole polynomial g_i(X) each round instead of just a number?
What is the verifier actually comparing g_i(0)+g_i(1) against — does it know the true value?
Where does the randomness live, and why does the prover not know r_i in advance?
Why does fixing one variable to r_i make the problem smaller by a whole variable?
If a liar can be lucky in one round, why is the whole protocol still trustworthy?
Why is there NO commitment or cryptography here when everything else in the book has it?
bad_intuitions_to_kill: ‘Verify a sum ⇒ recompute the sum.’ Replace with: the verifier checks a chain of one-variable consistency identities and one final evaluation, never the 2^n terms.

‘The verifier knows the true partial sums.’ Kill it: the verifier only checks g_i(0)+g_i(1) against the PREVIOUS round's reduced claim; the sole contact with the real g is the single final point g(r_1,…,r_n).

‘A lie can be repaired round to round.’ Kill via induction + Schwartz–Zippel: a wrong univariate differs from the true one as a non-zero low-degree polynomial, so a random r_i exposes it with prob ≥ 1 − d/|F|; surviving all rounds has prob ≤ d·v/|F|.
‘Sum-check needs a commitment.’ Kill by showing the entire soundness argument uses only Schwartz–Zippel and oracle access to g — unconditional, no primitive.
‘Per-round error compounds multiplicatively.’ Kill with the union bound: errors ADD to d·v/|F|, linear in v.
